usageContext: 'un mec' = a guy; 'mon mec' = my boyfriend; as address: 'ça va, mec ?'
'une meuf' = a girl; 'ma meuf' = my girlfriend. Fine among friends; can sound dismissive about a stranger.
warningNotes: Referring to a present woman you don't know as 'cette meuf' can come across as rude — context matters.

usageContext: the universal placeholder noun: 'c'est quoi ce truc ?'; also 'c'est pas mon truc' = not my thing

examples.translation: Hand me the thingy over there.

usageContext: casual meals among friends: 'on va bouffer ?'. 'La bouffe' = food/grub.
warningNotes: Don't use at a formal dinner or with your partner's grandparents.

examples.text: Y a rien à bouffer dans ce frigo.
examples.translation: There's nothing to eat in this fridge.

usageContext: 'je kiffe ce morceau' (I love this track); 'je te kiffe' = I'm into you
'c'est un truc de ouf' = that's insane (good or bad); 'un plan de ouf' = an amazing deal
'il est relou' (he's a pain), 'c'est relou' (that sucks/that's annoying)

examples.text: C'est relou, le métro est encore en grève.
examples.translation: It's such a pain, the metro's on strike again.

usageContext: 'un mec chelou' (a sketchy guy), 'c'est chelou' (that's weird)

examples.text: Il m'a envoyé un message chelou hier.
examples.translation: He sent me a weird message yesterday.

usageContext: the default casual word for work: 'au boulot !', 'j'ai trop de boulot'. Métro, boulot, dodo.

examples.text: Je te laisse, je retourne au boulot.

usageContext: 'ma bagnole est en panne' — everyday casual for car; 'caisse' is the younger synonym

examples.text: On prend ma bagnole ou la tienne ?
examples.translation: Are we taking my car or yours?

usageContext: 'j'ai acheté des fringues' — casual clothes shopping talk; verb 'se fringuer' = to dress

examples.text: Elle a toujours des fringues stylées.

usageContext: 'un pote' = a friend without the romantic ambiguity of 'ami(e)'; 'mon meilleur pote' = my best mate

examples.text: Je dîne avec des potes ce soir.
examples.translation: I'm having dinner with friends tonight.

usageContext: as an answer: 'Tu viens ? — Grave !' (totally!); as intensifier: 'il est grave sympa'

examples.text: C'était bien ? — Grave, faut y retourner !
examples.translation: Was it good? — Totally, we have to go back!

whoUses: absolutely everyone, to the point of national self-parody
usageContext: connective filler gluing sentences: 'il pleuvait, du coup on est restés'. Also restarts topics: 'du coup, tu viens demain ?'

examples.text: Le resto était fermé, du coup on a commandé des pizzas.
examples.translation: The restaurant was closed, so we ordered pizza.

usageContext: greeting among close friends in street register: 'wesh, ça va ?'. Strongly age- and identity-marked.
warningNotes: From a learner it almost always sounds like parody. Understand it, don't perform it.

whoUses: everyone under ~50 uses at least the classics
usageContext: syllable-flipping: femme→meuf, fou→ouf, lourd→relou, louche→chelou, arabe→beur. Some words got re-verlaned (meuf→feumeu).
warningNotes: Learn the established classics as vocabulary; don't generate new verlan yourself — real usage is conventional, not productive.

examples.text: « Chanmé » c'est le verlan de « méchant » — ça veut dire génial.
examples.translation: 'Chanmé' is verlan for 'méchant' — it means awesome.
